author: @leviathansnemesis3742
text: Where did you get a motor to work from such low voltage, is this a consumer product or self made?

replies.author: @BenjaminGoose
replies.text: Miniature toy helicopters/quadcopters contain tiny motors that can run on 400mV or so, or you can find them on eBay or Amazon usually listed as something like "DC 3.7V motor". Despite the listed 3.7V they can run down to a very low voltage.

Is this what your studies confirm ?

author: @peterdkay
text:
27 seconds to discharge time to 5% voltage is pretty severe punishment for a battery and will probably damage it.
If you want to get maximum power out of your battery, you should discharge into a load at least 10 times your internal resistance. It appears your internal resistance is about 10 ohms so your load should be greater than 100 ohms. That way at least 90% of the stored energy is available externally. Your motor appears to be about 15 ohms (1.2V/80mA) so only about 60% of your battery's energy is available externally.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not a battery - it's a super capacitor the power density can more than handle this

replies.author: @peterdkay
replies.text: Same response for a capacitor. i.e. The load must be 10 times internal resistance. If you do this you can get even higher energy density for your capacitor. In this case energy available will be 40% higher.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: What I think you are talking about is C rate discharge mate - nice rule of thumb you have and I will bear it in mind but what I would do is load the device at C/20, 1C, 5C, 10C and 60-80C if I really wanted to investigate the effect

replies.author: @peterdkay
replies.text:
"C" normally refers to batteries which have a high internal resistance compared with super-caps. A discharge at rate 1C means that you discharge it in 1 hour. A rating of 10C means you discharge it in 0 hour (6 minutes).  
I think using a load of 10 x internal resistance is a much better way to discharge your capacitor. This allows 90% of the available energy to be accessed externally.
In your case I calculated 10 ohms internal resistance so you should discharge it into >100 ohm load. 
A better solution is to purchase a constant current load like this one:
http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/Constant-Current-Electronic-Load-9-99A-60W-1-30V-Battery-Capacity-Tester-SU-/252773208350?hash=item3ada75111e:g:sOMAAOSwB09YP9ak
It only costs $15 and displays voltage and watt-hours during discharge.
You can then determine best discharge rate very quickly.

I would love to get hold of a few of these caps to test. I am willing to pay.
Keep up the good work! I am eager to see if you can commercialize this super cap.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate I don't think that's right - in my understanding C refers to the capacity of the active material -for example if a particular material is able to move an amount of electrons at a certain voltage - you need to refer to the pourbaix diagrams and stoichiometric equations to work it out for redox - for a given molarity of material you will get a given number of electrons at a given voltage - the molarity of material converts to a given weight and a capacity normalised to the weight - this is the C rate - resistance doesn't come into it at this stage - I did a video to explain C rates

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
This looks like an attractive replacement for lead acid in solar energy storage, where out gassing is already accepted and high self discharge rates are not a problem.
As an alternative to sealed lead acid it might find application as battery backup in telecom and computer systems.
A low cost, non toxic, carbon based battery must be very attractive to industry. I look forward to seeing the full battery specifications.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That was exactly my thought mate

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Have you considered defining your own case standard or using the existing lead acid battery case?
Is there any issue with the carbon igniting if it partly dries out? This carbon technology is certainly much safer than lithium batteries. If self ignition and self heat generation is not an issue, there seems no reason not to enclose it in a light weight, low cost polyethylene or polypropylene case. Another application is in the area of autonomous mobile robots, where there is a requirement for light weight, robust, high energy density batteries. Are you aware of the work on robots of Boston Dynamics?  You may find it interesting and potentially profitable to view the YouTube videos from this company.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I'll look it up mate - cheers

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith I hope you do not mind the link to Boston's Dynamics. The reason I mentioned them is because they appear to be leaders in the field of robotics. I wanted to show another area where light weight, high density energy packs are required. I believe that many of their autonomous robots are powered by Lithium battery packs. This is clearly a growing area as intelligent robots are starting to move from lab to industry. A major limiting factor is their very demanding power requirements. There is a growing market for robot toys and robot home assistants and drones. A long life, light weight, safe, non toxic battery offers a considerable advantage in this market over Lithium technology. Since these devices are charged just before use and most likely be put on float charge when not in uses, self discharge is not an issue. 

I use 5 APC UPS units to power my computer and telecom systems, these float charge   SLA batteries. Their weight is considerable, due to the lead content. The batteries fail after 3 to 5 years on float charge, and must be recycled. These appear to be an ideal target for replacement by a graphene super capacitor, since the UPS output is a synthesised approximate sine wave at mains voltage, there only requirement is to replace the up converter with one designed to work with a wide DC input range.
